title: OAuth 2.0 Implementation Risks: PKCE Misuse, Token Substitution, and Redirect Integrity
excerpt: Specialist review of real OAuth 2.0 implementation failures and robust patterns for redirect URI and token trust controls.
content:
# OAuth 2.0 Implementation Risks: PKCE Misuse, Token Substitution, and Redirect Integrity

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: identity engineers, API architects, application security teams
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 16 minutes

## Why This Matters
OAuth 2.0 vulnerabilities are frequently caused by implementation assumptions rather than protocol flaws. Misconfigured redirect URI validation, weak state handling, and token audience confusion can allow attackers to steal authorization codes or substitute tokens across clients.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: delegated access tokens, account data, privileged API scopes
- Entry points: authorization callback handlers, token exchange endpoints, mobile deep links
- Trust boundaries: browser redirects, client-app context, authorization server to resource server
- Typical attacker goals: account linking abuse, token theft, privilege escalation

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers abuse overly broad redirect URI matching, bypass weak state verification, and exploit client confusion between ID token and access token semantics. They target clients that skip PKCE for public flows or fail to bind token audience and issuer checks in resource servers.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: redirect URI exact matching and callback validation rules
- Dynamic checks: malicious callback permutations and authorization code replay tests
- Telemetry indicators: repeated failed callback exchanges and cross-client token anomalies
- False positive control: demonstrate token misuse impact on protected API resources

## Secure Implementation
Require PKCE for public clients, enforce strict redirect URI registration and exact comparison, and validate iss, aud, exp, and nonce or state context as applicable. Separate token types clearly and reject ID tokens where access tokens are required.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Exact redirect URI matching with no wildcard shortcuts
- [x] Mandatory PKCE for browser and mobile clients
- [x] Strong state and nonce validation at callback
- [x] Strict token audience and issuer checks in APIs
- [x] OAuth abuse-case tests in CI and staging

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: OAuth and authentication cheat sheet guidance
- CWE: CWE-346, CWE-613
- WSTG: authentication and session testing workflows
- ASVS: protocol and token validation controls

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/OAuth2_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7636
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
author: Security Team

title: Web Cache Deception and Key Poisoning: Detection and Defensive Routing
excerpt: A practical specialist guide to preventing cache deception and key poisoning across CDN and origin boundaries.
content:
# Web Cache Deception and Key Poisoning: Detection and Defensive Routing

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: web platform engineers and edge security teams
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
Caching delivers scale, but incorrect cache key design can expose private content and amplify attacker influence. Cache deception attacks often exploit route parsing mismatches between CDN and origin, causing sensitive dynamic responses to be cached and served to unauthorized users.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: authenticated pages, API responses, session-specific user data
- Entry points: edge routes, static extension handlers, cache-control behavior
- Trust boundaries: CDN cache layer to origin routing logic
- Typical attacker goals: data exposure and poisoning of high-traffic responses

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers craft path and query combinations that appear cacheable to the edge but dynamic to the origin. They exploit header normalization differences and vary requests to force shared cache entries for personalized responses. They also poison cache keys with unkeyed inputs that alter output content.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: cache key composition and route classification audits
- Dynamic checks: deception probes with authenticated and anonymous comparison
- Telemetry indicators: unusual hit ratios on endpoints expected to be private
- False positive control: verify cross-user response leakage in controlled tests

## Secure Implementation
Separate static and dynamic routes unambiguously, use strict cache key definitions, and ensure authenticated responses are never stored in shared caches. Normalize inputs consistently between edge and origin and pin cache-control directives for sensitive endpoints.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Explicit no-store for personalized responses
- [x] Cache key design reviewed for untrusted inputs
- [x] Edge and origin route normalization aligned
- [x] Cache poisoning test cases in regression suite
- [x] Monitoring for sensitive endpoint cache hits

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: configuration and data exposure risk guidance
- CWE: CWE-525, CWE-444
- WSTG: information leakage and misconfiguration testing
- ASVS: response handling and cache security controls

## References
1. https://portswigger.net/web-security/web-cache-deception
2. https://portswigger.net/web-security/web-cache-poisoning
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/
author: Security Team

title: Insecure Deserialization in Web Backends: Gadget Surface and Safe Patterns
excerpt: Specialist techniques for finding dangerous deserialization paths and replacing them with explicit safe contracts.
content:
# Insecure Deserialization in Web Backends: Gadget Surface and Safe Patterns

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: backend maintainers and secure code reviewers
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
Serialization frameworks are often trusted implicitly, yet unsafe deserialization can turn data ingestion into code execution or privilege escalation. Exploitability depends on available gadget chains, but impact can be severe even when direct RCE is not present.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: server integrity, sensitive data, internal control planes
- Entry points: session stores, message queues, file imports, API payload decoders
- Trust boundaries: untrusted serialized data to object reconstruction logic
- Typical attacker goals: remote code execution, auth bypass, data tampering

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers deliver crafted serialized payloads that trigger dangerous object graphs during reconstruction. They exploit polymorphic deserializers, unrestricted class loading, and custom hooks that execute side effects. They can chain these behaviors with other weaknesses to move laterally.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: inventory all deserialization entry points and accepted formats
- Dynamic checks: payload mutation and gadget-trigger testing under instrumentation
- Telemetry indicators: anomalous class instantiation and parser exception clusters
- False positive control: demonstrate controlled side effect from crafted payload

## Secure Implementation
Avoid native object deserialization for untrusted data and use explicit schema-driven formats. Apply strict type allowlists and isolate decoding layers from privileged runtime capabilities. Keep dependencies patched and remove legacy serialization pathways not required by product behavior.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Replace unsafe deserializers with strict schema decoders
- [x] Enforce type allowlists and deny unknown classes
- [x] Remove legacy object deserialization endpoints
- [x] Add deserialization abuse tests to CI
- [x] Runtime monitoring for suspicious decode paths

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: insecure deserialization category guidance
- CWE: CWE-502, CWE-915
- WSTG: server-side testing and input validation tracks
- ASVS: secure coding and runtime safety controls

## References
1. https://owasp.org/www-project-top-ten/2017/A8_2017-Insecure_Deserialization
2. https://portswigger.net/web-security/deserialization
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/502.html
author: Security Team

title: CSRF in Modern SPA Architectures: SameSite Limits and Token Binding
excerpt: Practical defenses for CSRF in API-driven SPAs where cookie auth, CORS, and browser defaults interact in subtle ways.
content:
# CSRF in Modern SPA Architectures: SameSite Limits and Token Binding

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: frontend and backend engineers maintaining cookie-authenticated APIs
- Difficulty: practitioner
- Estimated read time: 13 minutes

## Why This Matters
Many teams assume SameSite cookies fully solve CSRF. In reality, cross-site request behaviors, browser exceptions, and mixed authentication patterns can still enable state-changing abuse. CSRF remains critical in applications that rely on cookie credentials for API calls.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: account settings, payment methods, privileged state changes
- Entry points: state-changing API endpoints and hidden method override routes
- Trust boundaries: browser credential behavior to server-side action authorization
- Typical attacker goals: unauthorized actions via victim session context

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers lure victims to malicious pages that trigger forged requests using browser credential behavior. They exploit endpoints that skip CSRF token checks or trust CORS decisions as an authorization mechanism. They chain weak preflight protections with endpoint method confusion.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: endpoint inventory for mutation operations and token enforcement
- Dynamic checks: cross-site form, fetch, and image request variants
- Telemetry indicators: high-risk mutations without expected anti-CSRF token patterns
- False positive control: verify state change using only victim ambient credentials

## Secure Implementation
Use anti-CSRF tokens for all state-changing requests, apply strict same-site and secure cookie settings, and verify origin or referer as a secondary signal. Keep authorization independent from CORS and block unsafe methods for unauthenticated contexts.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] CSRF tokens on all state-changing endpoints
- [x] SameSite and Secure cookie policy reviewed per auth flow
- [x] Origin or referer validation for sensitive actions
- [x] Explicit method and content-type controls
- [x] CSRF abuse tests in automated security suite

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: CSRF prevention and session security guidance
- CWE: CWE-352, CWE-346
- WSTG: session and request forgery testing tracks
- ASVS: request integrity and session controls

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://portswigger.net/web-security/csrf
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html
author: Security Team

title: Detection Engineering for Web Attacks: Logging Design, Triage, and Containment
excerpt: Operational security blueprint for turning web telemetry into actionable detection and rapid containment outcomes.
content:
# Detection Engineering for Web Attacks: Logging Design, Triage, and Containment

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: SOC engineers, incident responders, platform owners
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 14 minutes

## Why This Matters
Preventive controls reduce risk, but detection quality determines whether attacks are stopped before impact scales. Many organizations log too much low-quality data and too little high-value context. Effective web detection engineering requires event design tied to attacker techniques and operational playbooks.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: user accounts, API integrity, sensitive records, availability
- Entry points: web and API edges, authentication services, admin interfaces
- Trust boundaries: application telemetry to SIEM and response workflows
- Typical attacker goals: persistence, data exfiltration, privilege abuse, disruption

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: identify high-signal events for auth, data access, and mutation paths
- Dynamic checks: adversary simulation and replay against detection rules
- Telemetry indicators: impossible travel, token reuse anomalies, suspicious error clusters
- False positive control: tune by route criticality, identity risk, and historical behavior

## Response Strategy
Define containment actions by confidence tier, including token revocation, session quarantine, and targeted rate controls. Build investigation timelines with correlated request IDs, user IDs, and infrastructure context. Use post-incident reviews to close detection gaps and add regression coverage.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] High-value security event taxonomy documented
- [x] Correlation IDs propagated across edge and backend systems
- [x] Rule quality metrics tracked and tuned continuously
- [x] Automated containment actions for confirmed abuse patterns
- [x] Post-incident learning loop tied to engineering backlog

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: logging and monitoring failures guidance
- CWE: CWE-778, CWE-223
- WSTG: operational verification and evidence quality
- ASVS: audit logging and security monitoring controls

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Logging_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
author: Security Team
